Report
Rescue Below the Moons of Eden by Elusive Faith
0 Reviews Table of Contents Download ePub Chapter or Story
[This is an unauthorized, unofficial sequel to "Under the Moons of Eden, by Christopher Leeson]

Years after the war with the Asymmetrics has ended, the Earth Alliance has sent out survey crews to map out new territories ceded as part of the cease fire. The crew of the Peregrine stumble upon a planet they couldn't have anticipated.
Categories: Sci-Fi Gender Change
Characters: Female to Male, Adult (27-62 yrs)
Motivation for Change: Forced
Sexual Orientation: Changes to be Attracted Only to Men
Story Theme: Distant Future, Fan Fiction, Romance
Type of Change: Fast/Instant Change, Stuck
Series: None
Chapters: 17 Completed: Completed Story
Word count: 62585 Read: 4775
Published: 04/12/26 Updated: 04/13/26
Story Notes:
I don't know if submitting this story here is even allowed. It's fanfiction--and unauthorized sequel to a classic story in the community. I read the original, "Under the Moons of Eden, by Christopher Leeson" many, many years ago and it became one of my favorite stories that I returned to multiple times.

I wrote this in 2015 as my submission for Nanowrimo and, since I have decided to try and submit more stories here, I figured I might as well leave this story here as an homage to the story that got me into this to begin with.

If I am overstepping by submitting this, I will remove it.
Author's Chapter Notes:
[This is an unauthorized, unofficial sequel to "Under the Moons of Eden, by Christopher Leeson]
##Chapter 1##

“That’s got to be one of the most beautiful sounds in the universe,” I mused–perhaps a little too loudly–to myself as I listened to the steady buildup of the Peregrine’s jump drives. That resonating vibration felt across the hull as the winding hum increased gradually in pitch like some Old-Earth opera singer reaching their crescendo made every month of this year-long mission worth it.

Through the forward bulkhead window, the bright blue star of the Theron system was suddenly washed-out, engulfed in a bright flash of light as the jump drives finally discharged their pulse and sent us rocketing through space towards our next destination. By the time the light cleared from my vision, I could hardly tell that we’d just travelled over twelve light years.

The only true indicator that we had moved anywhere was that Theron’s soft blue star that I had come to know over the past month was replaced in the window by a new, warm, bright yellow star.

Vetters.

If Harwell’s briefing yesterday was accurate–and, knowing him for the annoyingly precise man that he was–it was going to take the crew over a month to complete the arduous task of mapping all of the system’s celestial bodies. And not just mapping, either. Every major planetoid was going to need to be assessed for any valuable raw resource so that the Earth Alliance could properly plan how they were going to best reap Vetters.

Or, if the system was suitable enough, colonize it.

In accordance with our standard operating procedures, our entry into the Vetters system was at five astronomical units from the system’s center of mass and thirty degrees above the ecliptic plane. I placed the jump drive into idle and engaged stabilizing engines to keep us from drifting.

“Crew, this is your senior pilot, Major Doughty, jump complete, drive is idle.” My voice rang clear over the ship’s internal communications net, triggering the execution of our System Entry checklist.

A moment later came a reply, “This is captain Harwell, mission crew is ready, sensors are cyclic.”

The Peregrine ran on checklist execution like a carefully choreographed dance, each person having their moment to shine. Precision was key, but not because anyone felt particularly driven but rather because we all were keenly aware that our commander was listening closely to every step, looking for mistakes.

“Jump engines on cool down, entering diagnostic stage,” Came the chief engineer’s voice shortly after.

Finally, our ball-busting commander himself wrapped up the checklist with a crisp, to the point, call over the net, “Crew, begin sensor sweeping of the system, as briefed.”

Maybe I’m not quite being fair to the man. During the war, Lieutenant Colonel Bey was quite the celebrity–never afraid to fight the good fight. Top Brass would say he was the epitome of the perfect soldier, one who commanded a fire frigate with distinction across multiple campaigns. Leave it up to higher headquarters to use fifty-cred words like epitome–most soldiers just called it what it was.

Bey was a badass.

After all, only a badass would have the balls to take their fire frigate up against an Asymmetric capital ship and win. Those guys–the Assies–had quite the arsenal on their side and at times it seemed like they would surely overwhelm us, but time and time again, Bey managed to clutch multiple victories against them.

That’s why higher headquarters took an interest in him–even started to groom him to one day become a general like themselves. But if that had been his trajectory, then things must have gone askew given that he was still a Lieutenant Colonel three years after the ceasefire.

To this day, no one on the crew knows exactly why that was the case, but speculation ran rampant across both the officer and enlisted corps. I had it on good authority that Bey was accused of continuing to engage the Asymmetric Fleet even after the ceasefire was declared. While official charges were never filed against him, there must have been some credibility to the accusations given how quietly the Top Brass swept the matter under the rug and lost Bey’s number.

Being the commander of the 931st Deep Survey Patrol was a rung far lower than the command of a fire frigate–at least in Bey’s eyes, given how he seemed to overcompensate with a nigh obsessive adherence to rules and regulations.

I really wish the man would learn to pull the stick out of his ass at least one day a week.

Because, truthfully, this was a dream gig. Piloting the Earth Alliance’s state of the art Magellan class survey vessel was about the cushiest jobs a pilot like me could ask for next to shuttling generals around Sedna Prime.

Collectively we were the Peregrine, two-hundred and fifty-five soldiers tasked with performing long-duration exploration on behalf of the Earth Alliance. While a few of the crew were first-time deployers, the majority of us were veterans of the war with the Asymmetrics and we’d become quite accustomed to being far and away from the oppressive thumb of higher headquarters and Bey was black fly floundering in an otherwise delicious chowder.

Still, no matter how much I enjoyed being away from civilization, a man can only stay away for so long before he starts to miss the creature comforts available to an officer with twelve months worth of pay to burn.

Thankfully, Vetters was scheduled to be our final survey before the Peregrine was scheduled to return to port. Just a little more than a month of surveying and I would be looking at several months of complete freedom.

Speaking of freedom–now that the jump was complete, my shift was over, “Hey Kuch, I’m heading for bed, the Peregrine’s is yours.”

Lieutenant Brian Kucharski, one of only three men I had any actual direct responsibility for on this ship, glanced up from the checklist he still had his nose buried in and nodded, “Copy, my craft, Ian. Enjoy your nap.”

He hesitated, then added rather sheepishly, “And if you run into Nick along the way, tell him I’ll get his money once I hock my ball to one of the scope geeks.”

The kid had the worst luck, “You mean the one signed by Laramie Garret?”

Kuch nodded painfully, “Yes, that one.”

I slipped out of the piloting console and leaned across the center divide to offer the man a bit of a consolatory pat on the back, “You need to stop while you’re behind, Kuch. I’ve known Nick a long time–he just doesn’t know how to lose.”

+

“At this point, payroll should just cut out the middleman and send all of his checks directly to me,” Nick joked while leaning precariously back in his chair in the mess hall. “I love the guy, but damn, he’s a walking, talking case of bad luck.”

It’s been a long-running tradition throughout this deployment that the flight crew get together at the end of a long day to just shoot the breeze—except, of course, for the pilot left on duty, which at that moment was Kucharski. At four officers, we were by far the smallest division of the 931st, so it was only natural that we’d become as close as friends and not just coworkers.

The fourth among us, Trevor, smirked at Nick’s remark but found it pertinent to try and come to the young pilot’s defense, “He’s still young, Nick. Maybe some of that bad luck is just a case of inexperience—we’ve all been there before.”

“Not me,” Nick continued to muse. “And besides, if you’re calling him young, then what does that make you, Trev? Old?” He leaned forward, setting his chair right on the floor while scooping up his potato-laden fork to point at me, “And if you’re old, Ian here is practically ancient.”

I laughed, “By that logic, Nick, you're ancient, too. And if we're ancient, what do you suppose that makes Bey?”

As if he was expecting those exact words from me, Nick promptly blurted, “Prehistoric!”

As usual, our traditional get together was turning into Nick Craft’s comedy hour. If Kucharski was known for his bad luck, then Nick was best known for being able to turn just about any situation into a joke.

Our outburst of laughter following his punchline was loud, but it didn’t draw any particular attention from the others in the mess hall, so far as I could tell. The surrounding soldiers enjoying their own off-duty time were used to our antics by now. Rare was the time when two or more of us pilots got together and weren’t being loud.

Such was the life of a pilot—a different breed of soldier altogether. Marching in formations, ground battles, boarding actions? Those were all things better suited to men like Bey. We were nothing but glorified bus drivers, but at least we knew how to have a good time.

Rank in the service always has this need to create rigid hierarchies—officers and enlisted shouldn’t fraternize, always address another by their proper rank and last name, yadda yadda, yadda. With pilots, what mattered most was how good you flew and how entertaining your stories were. And while we as the flight crew recognized our bus-driver roll for what it was, all of us were good pilots.

And just because I had the good fortune of being senior pilot by virtue of my rank, it didn’t mean Nick and Trevor were just as capable—we’d all seen just as much action during the war, flying actual fighters, scouts, and even transports.

Nick drummed his hands on the edge of the table like he was trying to build up excitement for some announcement, “Alright gentlemen, it’s that time once more. We’re in a new system and that means a whole new round of wagers.”

Maybe I was hasty to characterize Nick as the funny man of group. He was that, yes, but across the entire crew he was more prominently known as the ‘Bookie’.

“And what have you got lined up for us this time?” Trevor asked, already pulling out a notepad of all his previous wins and losses.

“Well, for starters, how about we start with guessing how long it is going to take the scope geeks to finish the initial scan of the system? Winner is the closest in thirty-minute increments without going over.”

I shook my head, “I’ll pass.”

Nick shook his head right back, adding a taunting ‘tsk’, “Weak, Ian.”

“What can I say? I’m getting too ancient for this shit.”

“I think I know how we can spice things up a bit,” Trevor cut in, drawing Nick’s attention, “maybe instead of wagering creds, we try something a little more interesting?”

I could tell Nick’s interest had been piqued immediately despite his attempts to hide it under a casual shrug, “Gee Trev, all this time I had no idea my bets were putting you to sleep. But heck, I’m game—what do you have in mind?”

Trevor’s eyes darted between the two of us conspiratorially, as though he was preparing to reveal some sort of hidden truth of the universe, “Ever wagered a trump card?”

Nick and I shared a blank glance.

“Sounds like you just made it up,” I said before tipping back the last of my drink.

Undaunted, Trevor turned his focus to Nick, “It’s a real thing me and an old pal used do while we were in flight school. If you hold a trump card over someone, you get to pawn off a duty you don’t want—or get to steal one you do—to the other person.”

Nick gently stroked his chin, feeling the idea out, “Okay, sure, sounds interesting.”

“You sure you don’t want in on this, Ian?” Trevor asked, surely hoping that I would change my mind.

I stood, grabbing my tray as I prepared to head off, “What for? I get the ultimate trump card whenever I want—it’s called being the senior pilot.”

+

It felt like I had only just dropped onto my cot when my quarter’s communication panel began chirping like some early-morning robin. Even after blinking a dozen times, it was still difficult to read the red digital numbers of my bedside clock.

0530.

I reached out, fumbling around for the comm panel’s respond button and pressed it, “Major Doughty, go ahead.”

Nick’s voice was beyond giddy when it came across the line, “Good morning, Ian. It is my honest pleasure to report that the scope geeks have finished their initial scan as of 0524. Oh, and Senior Pilot, I also must inform you that the briefing is scheduled for the top of the hour.”

“Thanks,” already I was rolling—slowly—off my cot. “Sound like Trev lost the bet, then, huh?”

“Yup! Got myself a trump card!”

“Congratulations, try not to waste it on something trivial.”

Hastily, I dressed and made my way through the Peregrine’s halls to the main briefing room just outside of Lieutenant Colonel Bey’s quarters. While the rest of the ship’s décor kept functionality at the forefront of design, the briefing room had a whole pomp-and-circumstance feel about it—like that one room in your childhood home that was reserved for company that never seemed to use it even when they showed.

Maybe Bey thought that, at some point in time during our year-long survey out in the middle of nowhere, some general would show up and he wanted them to think the Peregrine was worthy of hosting top brass.

While I wasn’t late, I was the last to arrive and stepped in right behind Bey. As I worked past the large, walnut trimmed central table and took my seat next to Captain Harwell, I couldn’t help but notice I’d drawn the ire of Sergeant Major Grant—the Peregrin’s senior enlisted member.

It was a day ending ‘Y’, so that tracked.

He waited for me to make eye contact then made a whole silent show off looking at his wrist watch. The man was Bey’s personal cheerleader it seemed, always quick to remind officers and enlisted alike of Bey’s long list of wartime accomplishments. Funny, though, how he seemed to never mention Bey’s ceasefire ‘accomplishments’.

Grant took a seat next to Bey’s left while Major Trent sat to his right.

Trent was an okay guy by my own standards—certainly a typical officer type that preferred the hard separation between officer and enlisted and always quick to tow the company line. I’m pretty sure I had a week or so more time in service than him, which technically made me the senior officer, but Bey seemed keen on leaning on him more than myself.

So be it. My only real care was knowing Trent and the rest of the engineering team kept the engines together and working so I could get my flying fix.

The rest of the senior staff included Captain Prescott as the head of the small contingent of medical staff, and Captain Meyer who commanded the Peregrine’s security personnel—the only actual shoot-em-up soldiers assigned to the Peregrine.

“Start the brief, Captain Harwell,” Bey said in his typical stoic tone.

Harwell nodded, riding from his seat and tapping the sleek black surface of the table with his index. “Gentlemen,” the table’s surface came to life, displaying the entirety of the recently scanned Vetters system. “The star Vetters ranges in the high order K classification and we measure that it is roughly five billion years into its main sequence. Scans—”

“—Roughly, Captain?” Bey interjected. “I trust that your men didn’t just spend the past nine hours operating the Peregrine’s top-of-line sensors only to come up with a rough estimate of the stars age?”

I could feel my eyes start to roll. Thankfully, my better senses caught ahold of me before anyone was the wiser.

“No, sir,” Harwell stammered, “Apologies for not—”

Harwell’s hedging was just something else Bey could latch onto, “Don’t apologize, soldier, just get it right.”

Nodding, Harwell tapped the table a few more times, orienting himself to the more-detailed report prepared by his team, “Vetters is four billion, eight hundred thousand years old, accurate to within fifty thousand years—as tolerated per standard operating procedures, sir.”

Bey nodded, seemingly satisfied by Harwell’s specificity.

“Scans have detected and categorized five planet bodies—three gas giants and two terrestrial—along with two lightly dense asteroid belts.” The table display shifted, zooming in on the first of the gas giants.

Bey reached out to the table surface, swiping his hand in a long arch, effectively skipping over Harwell’s brief of the planets. This display bounced around wildly from planet to planet as it fast forwarded to the briefing’s next topic. “Tell us about the terrestrial planets, Captain.”

Harwell nodded, taking a moment to find his place in the brief, remaining calm and composed, “Vetters Alpha is roughly—I mean, the planet has a mass of point four three to that of Earth’s and is positioned point two astronomical units from the Vetters star. Initial scans indicate that there is no atmosphere due to the planet’s minimal gravity.”

That information didn’t seem to grab Bey’s attention in the slightest, “And Vetters Beta?”

“Vetters Beta is positioned within the star’s habital zone and has a mass of point eight one to that of Earth’s with indications of an atmosphere. My recommendation is that we begin the next phase of the survey there, sir.”

“Thank you, Captain, but if it is all the same to you, when I am ready for your recommendation, I will be sure to ask you for it and not a moment sooner.”

I wouldn’t blame an outsider to these proceedings if they concluded Bey was in a bad mood and simply taking his frustrations out on poor Harwell, but everyone in the room knew the truth of the matter—this was just how Bey was.

Tact was for top brass, soldiers shouldn’t need to be coddled.

After Vetters Beta, the brief continued for another hour as Harwell started to drone on about the less interesting aspects of the system. More than once I felt myself starting to nod off with the only saving grace was the feeling of Grant’s stern gaze upon me—apparently it was bad manners to get droopy-eyed while Harwell listed off all of the asteroids in Belt Two that had managed to overcome hydrostatic equilibrium.

But, as all things do, the end arrived. “A bit of a rough start, Captain,” Bey said, “but overall a thorough brief. And, based on your information, I feel it would be best to begin the next phase of the survey by investigating Vetters Beta.” Bey then turned tome, “Major Doughty, inform the flight crew to take the Peregrine to the planet. Cooridnate with Captain Harwell to establish the most efficient orbit to begin the scans.”

With our marching orders in hand, Bey stood, triggering everyone else to do e same, “Dismissed.”

Everyone filed out ahead of me, save for Harwell who was busy collecting his things following the brief. I barely managed to exit through the door before I heard my name.

“Major Doughty, may I have a moment?”

It was Sergeant Major Grant.

I forced down a stiff lump in my throat and nodded, “Of course, Sergeant Major. What’s on your mind?” It was a rhetorical question, surely he was going to harp on me about my ‘behavior’ during the meeting.

The man had a terrible poker face—it just ate him up that he couldn’t brace me against a bulkhead and reel into me like one of the enlisted members of the crew. But that didn’t mean he was harmless. He was still capable of offering polite, politically correct reminders on how things should be done, “I noticed that you were the last to arrive to the meeting, sir.”

Such passive aggression. Thankfully I was far more awake now and could properly mask my complete disinterest.

“I am sure that, as second in command, you would want to set a good example for the rest of the crew.”

This was double speak for, “If you’re ever late to another meeting I am going to see you have latrine detail for a month!”

Oh, to be an enlisted man under this guy’s purview.

Despite my carefully crafted mask of indifference, I failed to hide a smirk, and his jaw clearly stiffened, “I don’t think setting a good example is anything to laugh at, sir.”

Clearly this man wanted to engage in some form of verbal spar. He was just begging me to stoop low and pull rank in a blatant attempt disregard his faux polite words of concern, That way he could run off to daddy Bey, complaining how the big mean Major wasn’t taking him seriously.

Well, he wasn’t the only one who could play at politics, “I appreciate your insight, Sergeant, and I will endeavor to set a better example in the future.”

Grant let out a muffled grunt, searching for a reply.

He was too slow, “If that was everything, Sergeant, I’m going to deliver Lieutenant Colonel Bey’s orders to the flight deck.” Without waiting for a reply, I turned down the hall with a distinct lack of exigence.

+

Arriving at the flight deck, I dropped into the left side piloting console.

Nick looked over, practically grinning from ear to ear, “I’m winning bets, and my relief shows up an hour early to his shift. I should buy a lottery ticket.”

“A lot of good a lottery ticket is going to do for you a hundred light years from the nearest colony.” I pulled out my checklist and started flipping through the pages.

“True, but all the money I would get from winning would start earning me plenty of interest.” Somehow Nick always seemed to find the silver lining in every situation. He glanced over, noting the checklist, “What’s the plan, I-an?”

Reaching the appropriate checklist, I held it up to show my fellow pilot, “Checklist two-two-one. Bey wants us to check out a planet in the habitable zone.”

“Intra-system maneuvers, eh? And to the habitable zone?” Nick pulled out his checklist, “You know, I read the report on Vetters that the Assies provided. It didn’t say anything about a planet in the habitable zone.”

I shrugged, errors in the reports the Assies turned over was buying new, “Yes, Nick, we’re all well aware of what the report said should–or shouldn't in this case–be here.” Most of those reports were sparse and inaccurate, no reason to think that the presence of a planet in Vetters habitable zone should be surprising. “Is it any surprise that the Assies might have lied?”

“Maybe,” Nick agreed. “What do you want to bet that they did?”

“Win the lottery first, then I’ll entertain making long-shot bets with you.” I shook my head and punched up the coordinates for Vetters Beta into the navigation system before flipping the switch to the ship’s internal net, “Crew, this is Major Doughty, prepare for intra solar transit. All positions reference checklist two-two-one.”

A moment later, “This is Lieutenant Thompkins, main engines powered and ready.”

A subtle vibration ran the length of the Peregrine as the engine ignited. I placed my hands on the piloting controls.

Nick cleared his throat, “With all due respect, Major, it’s still my craft.” He grinned, gently waving my hands away from the controls. “You know me, I’ll never pass up an opportunity to pilot.”

I lifted my hands, holding them up high in mock surrender, “By all means, Captain.”

+

A day later the senior staff was once again summoned to the briefing room. The mission crew had managed to complete their scans of Vetters Beta in record time. Thanks to their enthusiasm I was now fifty creds lighter thanks to Nick finally managing to rope me into one of his wagers.

Hoping it would help lift my spirit, I timed my arrival to the briefing so I arrived just behind Bey again. The clear irritation on Grant’s face made it worth it.

“What do you have for us, Captain Harwell?” Bey asked after settling into his chair, perched right on the edge of the seat so his back never made contact with the backrest.

Harwell loaded the scan results onto the central table display, “This is a high resolution scan of Vetters Beta. The planet has an Earth-like atmosphere composed of sixty-nine percent nitrogen and twenty-six percent oxygen with trace elements making up the final five percent. The planet’s surface is sixty-five percent covered in ocean…”

It was another well rehearsed, intricately detailed brief of the planet that quickly approached the sixty minute mark with barely a pause for breath or water. This time, however, I was actually interested in what Harwell had to say–this was only the third habitable planet we'd encountered so far. Even though many of the numbers were a little too in the weeds for understanding, the gist of it all made Vetters Beta sound like a promising place to set down roots.

The planet was perfectly habitable for humans and was complimented by two spectacular moons.

Major Trent seemed to take the news with a grain of salt, specifically stroking his chin while looking over the data, “You would think a habitable planet would have been noteworthy enough for the Assies to mention in the report they handed over.”

A fair observation, and one that has the others nodding their heads. Captain Prescott was next to voice his concern, “And why would the Assies give up a system with a suitable planet without so much as a fuss?”

Then Captain Meyer threw his speculation into the mix, “Maybe there is something wrong with it.”

The room filled with a soft roar of chatter, each man offering a litany of likely–and unlikely–reasons the Assies might have kept this planet hidden from their reports. Focus on the meeting began to wander but want about to tolerate that. He reined the group back in before things got too out of hand, “Gentlemen, refrain from questions that are outside the scope of this brief. Our mission here isn’t to speculate on Asymmetric decision making processes, it is to thoroughly survey this system and report our findings back to higher headquarters.”

The room quieted as each man took his seat and focus steadily settled on the commander.

Bey waited for all eyes to be on him before continuing, “Another good brief, Captain, but if there is nothing else–”

“–Actually, sir, there is one more thing.”

For a moment it looked like Bey was about bite the man’s head off from across the room for interrupting him. Thankfully, he kept his cool, “Very well–continue.”

The screen on the table, which up until then had been a slowly rotating image of the planet, zoomed in towards a landmass in the northern hemisphere. It kept zooming in closer and closer, finally stopping when the image was close enough to the surface to reveal what appeared to be a network of roads.

Dirt roads, but roads nevertheless.

“It was really quite by chance that we discovered this, sir. Captain Leen noticed the anomaly during his shift and I gave the go-ahead to focus our sensors in the area for a high resolution scan.”

Everyone–Bey, Grant, me–leaned forward in our chairs to get a better look at the display as the zoomed-in image of the dirt roads was replaced by a highly detailed image.

Harwell continued even as everyone marvelled at the image, “It appears that Vetters Beta is not only suitable for habitation, but it is already inhabited.”

Prescott, ever the twitchy man, sprang out of his chair, “The Assies are living down there and they didn’t tell us about it?!”

“It could be a long lost Assie colony they were unaware of,” Trent speculated.

“Actually–” Harwell started but was cut off by Bey’s booming voice.

“Everyone settle down!” He pointed at Harwell, “Captain, have your men resume their normal scans of the planet and I will make note that the Asymmetrics are still occupying this system in direct contradiction to the armistice agreement. This isn’t our mess get to bogged down in, leave it to higher headquarters to decide how to handle this.”

“Sir–”

“–What are you not clear on, Captain?”

Harwell flushed, mouth gaping while he sought his voice, “T-the inhabitants are not Asymmetrics, sir–they’re human.”

Once more the room erupted in surprised shouts, but this time Bey was quick to shut it down with a swift pound of his fist onto the walnut trim of the table, “I said settle down!” He held his stern gaze, leveling it towards each of us one at a time until we all sank into the backs of our seats.

When the room was silent enough to hear hair grow, Bey sat back down. “Captain, are you certain?”

Harwell nodded, “Yes, sir.” He tapped on the screen and the image panned and zoomed further towards a plot of cultivated land. Even from the bird’s eye view of the image, the tops of human heads were plainly visible–no way any man could mistake a human for an Assie. “While there aren’t any electromagnetic signals emanating from the surface–that we can detect, at least–we are confident in estimating that these people are living in a preindustrial state.”

Bey stood silent for a long minute, hunched over the screen and staring at the image as if he were willing the human’s within to gaze up at him. Eventually, he slowly sat back down, one hand pensively stroking his chin, “If humans are living here, I want to know why.”

He turned his focus upon me, “Major Doughty.”

“Yes sir?”

“Major Doughty, I want you and Captain Harwell to come up with a suitable course of action for making contact with the surface. Captain Meyer, as head of our security force, you will assist them.”

He didn’t wait for a response from any of us, just lifted his head, “Dismissed.”
Next Page
